nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, rocky substrates, and reef rubble where film and turf algae are abundant.

decorPreferences: established live rock with film algae

diet.notes: Voracious grazer of film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and nuisance hair algae. In sparsely-algaed tanks, supplement with blanched spinach, nori sheets, or vegetable-based pellet foods to prevent starvation.

summary: The Astraea Snail is a small, peaceful Caribbean reef snail widely kept as a cleanup crew member in saltwater aquariums. It is commonly considered one of the most effective grazers of film algae, diatoms, and nuisance hair algae available to reef keepers.
careNotes:
The Astraea Snail (Lithopoma tectum) goes by several names in the hobby — including Astrea Snail, Astraea Turbo Snail, and Conehead Snail — and is still widely listed under the older scientific name Astraea tecta, which remains common in trade catalogs. It is commonly considered a beginner-friendly reef cleanup crew member: smaller and less disruptive than true Turbo snails, so it is less likely to knock over corals and frags while still grazing efficiently on film algae, diatoms, cyanobacteria, and short hair algae.

CRITICAL HUSBANDRY NOTE: Astraea Snails are notoriously poor at righting themselves once knocked upside-down, whether by tankmates, water flow, or their own awkward climbing. A flipped Astraea will slowly starve and die over a few days if not turned right-side-up by the aquarist. Daily visual checks and prompt manual righting are essential — this is the single biggest cause of premature death in this species.

Like all marine invertebrates, they are highly copper-sensitive and cannot tolerate exposure to copper-based medications. They also decline rapidly in tanks with elevated nitrates or unstable salinity. Drip-acclimate slowly over one to two hours when adding them to a new tank to avoid osmotic shock (the sudden shift in salt concentration that can stress or kill marine invertebrates).

Astraea Snails are generally considered fully reef-safe with corals, but predatory tankmates — pufferfish, triggerfish, large wrasses, predatory hermit crabs, and stone or emerald crabs — will attack them. Stock at roughly one snail per four to eight liters of tank volume, adjusted for the actual algae load.
breedingNotes: The Astraea Snail reproduces through broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm directly into the water column. The resulting veliger larvae are planktonic — free-floating — and must feed in open water to develop through to settlement, a process that cannot be replicated in a home reef aquarium. Captive breeding has not been documented as a hobby achievement.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters (temp 24-25.5C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.025-1.026, dKH 8-9.5), reef-safe and care-level confirmation, and Caribbean origin.

additional.name: WoRMS - Lithopoma tectum
additional.notes: Confirmed accepted taxonomy: Lithopoma tectum (Lightfoot, 1786), family Turbinidae, order Trochida. Astraea tecta is listed as a synonym.

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty - The Astraea Snail (Astraea tecta)
additional.notes: Third source for size (~1-1.5 in / up to ~4 cm), lifespan (2-3 years), and the flip-and-cannot-right warning.
